turns.user_message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've tried before and it didn't work out. I'm not sure I'm ready to do this again.
step2_rag_chunks: Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

turns.user_message: I just couldn't do it. I'd get stressed or anxious, and I'd need a cigarette to calm down. And then I'd feel like a failure because I couldn't quit. Plus, I have friends who smoke and they're fine, so I don't think it's that big of a deal for me personally.
step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?

step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Exercise can help improve mood and reduce nicotine cravings.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

step2_rag_chunks: After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Planning ahead for difficult situations such as parties or social events can help prevent relapse.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
